data.title: Western Alliance Bancorp: The Easy Money Has Been Made, But More Upside Is Justified
data.description:
One of my better calls over the last few years now has involved Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL). Since I last called it a ‘buy’ back in July of 2023, the stock has risen 70.5%. That's comfortably better than the 53.5% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's not all. Since I originally turned bullish on it in April of 2023, the stock has risen an astounding 113.9%. The market, meanwhile, is up only 68.2% since then. Fundamentally speaking, the company seems to be doing quite well right now. Deposits continue to expand, and profitability is rising nicely. Asset quality is strong, and the stock actually looks fairly attractive in relation to earnings. When you add all of this together, I think it is premature to downgrade the business at this time.

This is not to say that this view could not change. It just so happens that new data will soon be coming out. After the market closes on April 21st, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. However, revenue is forecasted to rise. Interestingly, analysts believe that profitability for the company will contract. But given how cheap the stock is, even a small decline in earnings is not enough to derail my overall bullish assessment of the business. It would take a significant amount of pain to do that.

It’s still worth banking on Western Alliance Bancorp

Over the last three fiscal years, things have gone quite well for Western Alliance Bancorp. The company has seen its deposits expand from $55.33 billion in 2023 to $77.16 billion last year. This overall growth is encouraging. And what's really impressive is that, even as deposits expanded by 16.3% from 2024 to 2025, organic deposit growth had to have been higher. I say that because, during that window of time, high-cost brokered deposits that are borrowed from other financial institutions actually contracted from $5.6 billion to $4.3 billion. This is encouraging in and of itself, as it indicates that the organic picture for the company is solid. This is not to say that everything about the institution is great. Even as brokered deposits declined, uninsured deposit exposure increased. The firm went from having $17.6 billion of these in 2024 to $22.9 billion in 2025. But the good news is that this still means that uninsured deposit exposure amounts to no more than 29.7% of all deposits. I typically like to see this number come in at 30% or lower, so the firm just barely crosses under that threshold.

As deposits increased, the value of loans on the company's books grew also. From 2023 through 2025, loans jumped from $49.96 billion to $58.22 billion. The most significant exposure that the institution has here would be the $27.93 billion, or 47.6% of total gross loans, dedicated to commercial and industrial loans. A distant second would be the 25%, or $14.65 billion, tied up in residential real estate loans. The firm also has some exposure to other categories like non-owner-occupied commercial real estate, construction and land development loans, owner-occupied commercial real estate loans, and even a very small amount of consumer loans.

This is not the only area of expansion that the company has demonstrated. Over the last three years, we have seen a near doubling in the value of securities from $12.71 billion to $20.44 billion. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, surged from $1.58 billion to $3.60 billion. And debt actually contracted from $8.13 billion to $6.32 billion. What this shows is that management is reducing leverage while pushing capital into not only higher-yielding opportunities but also toward bolstering its cash position. This is a nice mix between offensive and defensive strategy aimed at reducing risk without missing out on upside.

My only complaint about the company is that, even as it has made some positive changes to its balance sheet, its net interest margin has contracted. It dropped from 3.63% in 2023 to 3.51% in 2025. And even though the business should benefit from declining interest rates because of its ability to immediately slash how much it is paying depositors, a sensitivity analysis conducted by the firm indicated that, if interest rates drop by 100 basis points, we should see a decline in net interest income ranging between 2.7% and 4.1%. A 200 basis point drop, depending on if it is done gradually or if it happens in short order, would translate to a decline of between 5.7% and 8.1%. Considering my own view that the economy will require additional interest rate cuts as economic conditions deteriorate, this is not exactly an ideal position for the business to be in.

Even so, up until this point at least, things have gone quite well. The increase in the size of the company's balance sheet has made possible a rise in revenue and profitability. In the chart below, you can see net interest income, non-interest income, and net profits, all over the past three years. The most important thing to me is the fact that net profits have risen from $709.6 million to $956.2 million. However, we would be foolish to ignore the surge in non-interest income during this time. Over the last three years, it has jumped from $280.7 million to $678.2 million. Admittedly, some of this was because, in 2023, the company had to book a fair value loss adjustment. That was worth $116 million. It also incurred $40.8 million in losses on the sale of investment securities. However, over the last three years, the firm has seen significant growth when it comes to its service charges and fees. Those have nearly doubled from $110.3 million to $194.3 million. Meanwhile, the net gain on mortgage loan origination and sale activities surged from $193.5 million to $255.5 million. On the whole, this is a positive for the business.

If we use the data from 2025, we can see in the first chart below how the company is priced on a price-to-earnings basis. The 8.2 reading that we get here is below the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. It also is cheaper than any of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. The subsequent chart below that shows the same type of analysis using the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, two of the five firms I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. And on a price-to-tangible-book basis, it was the cheapest of the group.

So far, things are looking good from a valuation standpoint. But that's not where the good news ends. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading for the institution of 1.12%. That's higher than in either of the previous two years. It's also above the 1% minimum that I typically like to see. Three of the five institutions that I compared it to are worse off than it is in this regard. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see return on equity. That's even better. The 13.3% reading that we get here is comfortably higher than the 10% minimum I prefer. And it puts the company above four of the five institutions I stacked it up against. This is not to say that there aren't things that I don't like. For instance, even though we have seen an improvement over the last 12 months, the firm has reported that 0.85% of its loans are classified as non-accrual. This is above the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. But on its own, it's not a deal breaker.

Pay attention to earnings

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management will soon be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. That's expected to occur after the market closes on April 21st. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will climb to $959.4 million. That's up from the $778 million that the business reported a year earlier. However, earnings per share are forecasted to fall from $1.79 to $1.63. That would imply a decline in net profits from $195.9 million to $178.5 million. Even if we were to annualize that, we would still be looking at a business that is trading on the cheap.

One of the biggest things to look out for when management announces financial results in the coming days will be any sign of improvement in net interest margin. As I detailed already, the company has seen a drop in this over the last three years now. And management has forecasted that a declining interest rate environment will negatively impact overall profitability here. While inflation has been running hotter than what the Federal Reserve is aiming for, and that could mean that interest rates remain higher for longer, there is always the prospect that worsening economic conditions could cause rates to fall faster.

Even though management has made it clear what the impact of declining rates would be, it is also true that, conceptually, declining rates allow banks to cut how much they are paying depositors almost instantly. So I would be interested to see more regarding management’s view of economic conditions and how their strategy toward dealing with this uncertainty is evolving. Obviously, a decline in profitability would hurt the valuation argument here. But unless profits plunge, which I do not see happening in the near term, I'm not terribly concerned there. In order to be even fairly valued, net profits for the company would have to drop 18% compared to what they were last year.

We should also be on the lookout for changes in credit quality. If the economic climate does become more painful, there is a real chance that the institution could see a rise in delinquencies and defaults. We have seen a bit of worsening here. From 2024 to 2025, the firm went from having 0.65% of assets classified as non-performing to having 0.69% matching that description. Both numbers were higher than the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see, but I wouldn't necessarily call them scary. But we have also seen a rise in net loan charge offs to average loans outstanding from 0.18% to 0.24%. So the trends here certainly matter, and I think investors would be smart to pay attention to how these data points continue to evolve. Continued worsening would definitely be one reason to consider downgrading the stock.

Takeaway

As tempting as it is to downgrade Western Alliance Bancorp after its historic run higher, I would say it's probably premature to do so. Although analysts have mixed expectations when it comes to the upcoming earnings call, the business has demonstrated itself to be a high quality operator that has been capable of expanding. Shares are cheap, and asset quality is robust. When you add all of this together, it makes sense to me to maintain it as a ‘buy’ candidate.

data.title: This Week's Market Wrap: War Fades, Markets Rip, Fed Hopes Rise
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava

War Fade Fuels Melt-Up

The dominant driver of markets this week was not just geopolitics, but the change in trajectory of those headlines. What began as a high-risk escalation between the U.S. and Iran quickly transitioned into a narrative of containment, and ultimately, de-escalation. That shift - particularly through the lens of oil - triggered one of the strongest two-week rallies in recent years.

At the start of the week, markets were on edge. President Donald Trump’s announcement of a blockade of Iranian ports, coupled with failed ceasefire negotiations over the weekend, sent oil prices surging above $100 per barrel and equity futures sharply lower.

Yet, that early weakness was short-lived. As reports surfaced of potential renewed negotiations - including a possible in-person meeting between U.S. and Iranian officials - equities reversed course aggressively.

By Tuesday, the tone had shifted decisively. Oil prices collapsed nearly 8% in a single session, settling near $91 per barrel, as the market grew confident that supply disruptions would be temporary. This decline in energy prices acted as a release valve for inflation fears and interest rate expectations, unleashing broad-based equity buying. Growth stocks led the charge, with Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META) all posting strong gains amid renewed risk appetite.

The momentum accelerated midweek. The S&P 500 broke to new all-time highs, driven largely by mega-cap leadership. Microsoft (MSFT) surged alongside software names like ServiceNow (NOW) and Datadog (DDOG), as investors rotated back into high-growth segments. Even as some semiconductor names lagged following cautious guidance from ASML (ASML), the broader trend remained intact.

The decisive catalyst came Friday. Iran’s announcement that the Strait of Hormuz would reopen to commercial traffic during the ceasefire triggered a sharp collapse in oil prices - down more than 10% to roughly $81 per barrel mid-day. This single development ignited a surge across cyclical sectors. Airlines like United Airlines (UAL) jumped nearly 9% mid-day, while cruise operators such as Royal Caribbean (RCL) posted double-digit gains.

The result was a powerful risk-on move that extended a two-week rally, with major indices not only recovering losses tied to the Iran conflict but also pushing into record territory. Importantly, the market’s ability to look past peak geopolitical risk - and instead price in resolution - was the defining feature of the week.

Investment Implication: Markets are increasingly trading on the direction of geopolitical risk rather than the absolute level, meaning inflection points in oil and conflict narratives can drive outsized moves across equities, rates, and inflation expectations.

Earnings Strength Meets Selective Scrutiny

As the geopolitical backdrop improved, first-quarter earnings season began to take center stage - and early results confirmed a key theme: fundamentals remain strong, but the bar for upside is high.

According to FactSet data, S&P 500 earnings are expected to grow 12.6% year-over-year, marking a sixth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth, with the potential to reach 19% as the season progresses. This backdrop provided a fundamental anchor for the rally, even as macro uncertainty persisted.

The financial sector offered one of the clearest signals of underlying strength. Goldman Sachs (GS) delivered better-than-expected results, driven by strong investment banking and record equities trading revenues, though the stock sold off on “sell-the-news” dynamics after a strong pre-earnings run. Morgan Stanley (MS) also impressed, posting strong trading and wealth management results, while Citigroup (C) stood out with upside net interest income guidance.

However, not all banks participated. Wells Fargo (WFC) declined sharply after missing revenue expectations, highlighting a growing dispersion within financials.

Technology and AI-related names continued to dominate the narrative. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) reported a 58% jump in profits, reinforcing the strength of AI-driven demand, even as it guided slightly lower on near-term revenues. Meanwhile, Broadcom (AVGO) rallied after expanding its partnership with Meta to support AI infrastructure, signaling continued capital spending momentum.

In consumer and industrial segments, results were mixed but instructive. PepsiCo (PEP) delivered a strong quarter with a return to volume growth in North America, while Abbott Laboratories (ABT) declined despite beating estimates due to weak forward guidance. Similarly, Netflix (NFLX) dropped nearly 10% after disappointing Q2 guidance, underscoring the market’s intolerance for forward-looking weakness.

Corporate activity also added to the narrative. Reports of a potential merger between United Airlines and American Airlines (AAL) drove both stocks higher, while Charles Schwab (SCHW) fell sharply after announcing a disruptive crypto platform, highlighting how strategic pivots can impact sentiment.

The market is placing a premium on companies with clear, durable growth visibility while penalizing any indication of margin compression or weakening demand. The emphasis now shifts to forward guidance, as investors look beyond the Q1 energy-driven volatility and focus on how management teams are adapting to these conditions going forward.

Investment Implication: Earnings season is confirming a more selective market where valuations alone aren’t enough - stocks are reacting to forward guidance, capital allocation, and exposure to durable themes like AI.

A key focus remains hyperscaler spending: investors want to see clear returns on that investment, or a shift toward discipline. Notably, many AI infrastructure names saw negative reactions during the Q4 2025 earnings season, underscoring this concern.

Data Holds the Line

While geopolitics and earnings dominated headlines, the economic backdrop played a critical supporting role - particularly in shaping interest rate expectations.

Inflation data provided one of the most important signals of the week. March Producer Price Index (PPI) came in well below expectations, with headline PPI at 0.5% versus 1.2% expected and core PPI at just 0.1%. This suggested that, outside of energy-driven spikes - particularly gasoline prices, which surged 15.7% - underlying inflation pressures remain contained.

This dynamic allowed markets to partially “look through” the oil shock, reinforcing the idea that inflation tied to geopolitical disruptions may be transitory rather than structural.

At the same time, labor market data remained firm. Weekly initial jobless claims declined to 207,000, continuing to signal a resilient employment environment. This strength is critical, as it supports consumer spending and reduces the risk of a demand-driven slowdown.

However, not all data points were constructive. Housing remained a clear weak spot, with existing home sales declining 3.6% month-over-month to a 3.98 million annual rate. The report cited higher mortgage rates, elevated prices, and limited inventory as ongoing constraints. Similarly, the NAHB Housing Market Index fell to 34, reflecting continued builder pessimism.

Industrial data also showed softness, with March industrial production declining 0.5%. While this was offset by upward revisions to prior months, it still points to uneven momentum in the manufacturing sector.

On the positive side, regional manufacturing surveys surprised to the upside. The Philadelphia Fed Index surged to 26.7, well above expectations, indicating pockets of strength despite broader industrial headwinds.

Taken together, the data paints a picture of an economy that is slowing in interest-rate-sensitive sectors but remains resilient overall - particularly in labor and services.

Investment Implication: The economic backdrop is keeping rate expectations in focus, particularly around whether the Federal Reserve delivers one or two cuts this year. With inflation data showing signs of containment - especially outside of energy - markets have room to price in an additional cut if that trend persists. At the same time, resilience in the labor market helps support this outlook by limiting downside risks to growth while giving the Fed flexibility in its policy path.

Chart of the Week

This week, we examine sector performance during the market’s recovery over the past 10 trading days. Leadership has clearly come from technology, as investors appear less concerned that elevated energy prices will materially compress margins in the sector compared to more cost-sensitive areas like consumer discretionary. At the same time, the ongoing buildout of AI infrastructure continues to provide a durable, long-term growth narrative that is attracting capital.

Relative to the S&P 500, leadership has been concentrated in technology, utilities, real estate, energy, and communication services. Consumer Discretionary has modestly lagged, while Materials, Industrials, Financials, Health Care, and Consumer Staples have either underperformed or posted outright declines over the same 10-day period.

Source: StockCharts.com

Bottom Line

This week’s market action was defined by a powerful interplay between geopolitics, earnings, and macro data. The de-escalation of the Iran conflict and resulting collapse in oil prices unlocked a surge in risk appetite, while early earnings results confirmed that corporate fundamentals remain intact but increasingly differentiated. Meanwhile, economic data continues to thread the needle between resilience and moderation.

The key shift is that markets are no longer reacting to absolute risks, but to changes in those risks. That transition - from escalation to stabilization - is what powered equities to new highs and will likely remain the dominant force shaping price action in the weeks ahead as investors turn towards earnings results from geopolitics.

Original Post

data.title: NCV: Dividend Coverage Improved But High Interest Rates Threaten Growth
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund (NCV), I issued a hold rating due to the questionable dividend coverage at the time. Since that last coverage, the fund has experienced some slight upside as investors rotated out of technology sector and into other areas of the market. Thankfully, NCV has been able to provide a total return that is slightly higher than the S&P 500 since then. The fund has now released an updated annual report and this prompted me to revisit the fund's value proposition, outlook, and performance expectations.

When I previously covered the fund, NCV traded at a discount to NAV of 10.72%. Despite the fund having a bit of a rally in the first quarter of 2026, the fund still trades at a very similar discount to NAV of 10.62%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that NCV trades at a fairly reasonable price to NAV level in relation to its historical range. For instance, the fund has traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.19% over the last five years. Despite the discount to NAV valuation, I am unsure about how appealing it is to accumulate shares at this time. The fund's growth potential still seems a bit limited at this time since interest rates remain elevated.

NCV now offers investors a starting dividend yield around 10%, while distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. The fund's latest annual report does reveal that earnings support those payouts with ease, but the fund's dividend history is a bit spotty. Ultimately, I believe that the fund's payouts are a bit too generous and this is stealing from the future returns of the portfolio. NCV's structure is reliant on its ability to capture upside growth from its holdings and then selling those gains. Over a long time frame, this has resulted in NAV erosion and reduced payouts.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, NCV now has total managed assets of $610.5M that are spread across a diversified range of assets. As implied by the fund's name, NCV invests in a portfolio of convertible securities and fixed-income positions that can provide a unique blend of capital appreciation and income potential. Convertible securities typically act as a hybrid tool that allows management to initially collect income from the position, but then convert that investment into common shares during periods of growth. However, investors need to remember that NCV prioritizes income generation above everything, so there should be limited expectations around its ability to provide capital appreciation. NCV's portfolio is broken down by the following structure:

  • Convertible Securities: 60.42%
  • High Yield Bonds: 32.85%
  • Cash & Equivalents: 4.01%
  • Other: 2.72%

The fund will typically aim to invest at least 50% of total assets into convertible securities but the allocation can vary based on market conditions and management's outlook on the market. If management believes that a market rally be occur, the portfolio may convert more positions into common shares. Furthermore, NCV is most heavily allocated to information technology positions at 24.08% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrials and financials, which account for 12.54% and 12.10% of assets respectively.

When looking at the top holdings, we can see many notable names here within the portfolio. However, it is important to remember that NCV maintains these positions through different convertible securities, which means that upside growth is pretty limited. The fund's top holding is current Lumentum Holdings (LITE) at 5.66%. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable companies, including Wells Fargo (WFC), NextEra Energy (NEE), Welltower (WELL), and the Boeing Company (BA), just to name a few.

The software sector has experienced headwinds with the growth of AI threatening the moat for many companies. While NCV is a bit shielded from these declines, there are still risks associated with its portfolio being most heavily allocated to the technology sector. While the exact allocation to software companies isn't highlighted, this could suppress the fund's valuation if sentiment of the market doesn't improve. Furthermore, NCV has a sizeable allocation to investments with below investment grade credit ratings.

Exposure to below investment grade borrowers can introduce some sensitivity to the threat of defaults. Just for reference, securities with a credit rating below BBB are classified as below investment grade and have historically been associated with higher rates of defaults. However, complete transparency into the fund's breakdown by credit rating is difficult to determine since NCV has so many securities that aren't rated. The portfolio's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 15.7%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 36.99%
  • Not Rated: 47.31%

Updated Performance Insights & Risks

Since the time of my last coverage, NCV has released an updated annual report that provides transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.54 per share, while the net realized gains landed at $3.18 per share. Looking back over prior years, we can see that net investment income has typically hovered in the same range over the last three years. However, net investment income has substantially declined from its prior levels between 2021 and 2022. This reduction can likely be attributed to the higher interest rate environment, which negatively impacts NCV's operating spread.

Net investment income is made up of the dividends and interest income that's received from positions. Since NCV is most heavily allocated to convertible securities, I would expect the amounts to be higher. Therefore, I would love to see net investment income levels substantially improved going forward so that they can support the distributions on their own.

The current dynamic means that NCV is heavily dependent on being able to capture a meaningful level of capital appreciation from its positions. While this is fine, it also introduces a vulnerability to market downturns. If the fund isn't capable of producing positive net realized gains during declines, NCV may find itself in a scenario where it is actively paying out more than it earned.

This was the case in 2022, which ultimately caused the NAV to decline and the fund still hasn't fully recovered from this decline. If net investment income supported distributions by itself, the fund would experience a lot better NAV resilience during unfavorable market conditions. Just for reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $25.84 per share
  • 2022: $22.20 per share
  • 2023: $16.32 per share
  • 2024: $14.72 per share
  • 2025: $15.74 per share
  • 2026: $17.58 per share

Although NCV has the power to convert its positions its common shares, this is offset by the fact that the fund relies on net realized gains. This is because the fund has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions and this prevents its positions from growing without interruption over time. So when measuring NCV's performance, we can see that the share price has declined by more than 28% over the last decade, despite market indices rallying to all-time highs. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 123.7% over the same time frame.

From a risk perspective, investors should be aware that NCV utilizes leverage as part of its operating strategy. The inclusion of leverage introduces a sensitivity to fluctuations in interest rates and can amplify downside movements. For instance, the fund may use leverage to buy a position that declines in value. This would accelerate the pace of the NAV declines and make it more difficult for capital appreciation to occur. The total effective leverage now amounts to 32.73% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$199.7M. In this higher interest rate environment, I would consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage.

Even if the leverage isn't allocated to positions that decline in value, it can still negatively impact earnings in this higher interest rate environment. For instance, a high cost of debt means that NCV's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities is thinning. This is likely the reason why we see net investment income substantially trend lower over the last few years. So in a scenario where interest rates remain high over the next twelve months, this can continue to suppress NCV's share price. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged.

Even worse, a scenario where interest rates rise would likely cause the share price to decline. This was demonstrated through 2022's market decline, which also showed us that a high yield does not equal a high return. While the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) experienced a total loss of 18.17%, NCV saw a much larger total loss of 34.08% over the same period, despite offering a double-digit dividend yield.

Declining Dividends

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.136 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10%. Assuming NCV keeps the distributions unchanged over the next twelve months, we can estimate an annual payout amount of $1.632 per share. Referring to the same annual report for the year, the fund has demonstrated that it is capable of generating sufficient earnings, however the track record is a bit spotty. The fund has typically struggled to generate the necessary level of earnings during periods where interest rates fluctuated or where market indices pulled back. However, coverage should be fine for the next twelve months.

2025's earnings of $3.47 per share / $1.632 annual payout = 2.12 years.

However, the fund has a history of declining dividends that date back before the pandemic. This can also be an indication that something is structurally wrong with the fund. I believe that the fund would benefit from a small reduction because it would reduce the pressure of needing to generate high income, while also provide a bit more NAV stability over time. For instance, a 20% reduction in payouts would still leave NCV with a starting dividend yield around ~8%, which is still very competitive among the high-yield landscape.

One of the issues is that the fund has been unable to grow its NAV over the last decade. The NAV did rapidly rise through 2020 and 2021, but this was an outlier driven by circumstances of the market at that time. The continuous downtrend of the NAV further reinforces that the payouts are a bit too generous. So while the fund can be an income producer for investors, the approach is a bit flawed and may be stealing from the fund's future growth potential. This concept makes it very difficult to recommend NCV for investors seeking a reliable source of supplemental income within their portfolio.

Lastly, we get confirmation of the fund's challenge generating earnings when looking at the section19(a) notice for the latest payout. It is revealed that most of the payout was classified as return of capital, which does offer such tax-efficiency but comes directly from the NAV. Return of capital is typically relied on when the fund fails to generate earnings to support payouts. Every time a return of capital distribution is paid, the NAV will be reduced by an equivalent amount.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that NCV remains a hold at this time. The fund does trade at a reasonable price to NAV valuation, but I believe growth potential remains limited because of the higher interest rate environment and the use of leverage. The latest annual report reveals that the fund is capable of generating sufficient earnings but the structure is a bit flawed. For a convertible fund, I would expect net investment income levels to be much higher so that the success of the fund isn't reliant on net realized gains. Furthermore, the distribution policy may bit a bit too generous since it has historically slowed the rate of NAV growth over the last decade. The overall appeal of the fund would improve once interest rates are lower and the NAV trends higher.

data.title: Regions Financial: Q1 Review, Credit Improvement Continues
data.description:
Shares of Regions Financial (RF) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 46% as the broader market has rallied from tariff-fear lows. In particular, the company’s credit quality results have been better than many feared, and this trend continued in a solid Q1 earnings release. With banks’ exposure to private credit gaining more attention, this was an encouraging outcome. I last covered Regions in October, rating the stock a “hold,” and this was too timid with shares rallying 18% since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit RF.

In the company’s first quarter, Regions Financial (RF) earned $0.62, which was $0.03 better than expectations as revenue grew 5% to $1.9 billion. Looking first at its balance sheet, average deposits were $130.2 billion. A prior concern of mine was that RF’s deposit growth has been poor, but we have seen meaningful improvement here. Balances are up about 2% from last year, primarily due to strength in corporate balances. Rates declined 9bps in the quarter, reflecting a pass-through of Q4 Fed rate cuts, and I expect costs to be roughly flat in Q2-Q3 vs current levels.

On the asset side, loans were $96.4 billion on average in Q1. The past year has been difficult to generate loan growth given high rates and tariff uncertainty, and RF’s balances have barely budged from a year ago While there has been a concern that geopolitical uncertainty could weigh on business sentiment and reduce loan demand, that risk does not appear likely to materialize soon for RF. In fact, its loan pipeline is up 21% from last year. This should enable low single-digit loan growth in 2026 with an increasing mix of business lending at the expense of consumer balances.

Net interest income (“NII”) was $1.26 billion, down sequentially given 2 fewer days. RF’s net interest margin was 3.67%, up 15bps from last year as fixed security reinvestment has boosted margins. NIM declined sequentially as Q4 was distorted by a dividend and surprise recovery of a nonaccrual loan. On a normalized basis, NIM was up 3bps sequentially, and I expect ~2bps of quarterly expansion through year end.

Like many banks, Regions has been stuck with bonds and swaps locked in when rates were much lower, limiting interest income and saddling it with unrealized losses until they mature. It has a $33 billion securities portfolio with a $1.2 billion unrealized loss that will take multiple years to roll down, but as it does, it can deploy that capital at higher yields to boost revenue. Earlier in April, RF sold $900 million of securities and reinvested them at current market levels. This caused a $40 million loss (which will slightly reduce capital) but generate ~$20 million of incremental income. This will boost NII and NIM slightly.

Additionally, it has $24 billion of fixed rate swaps at 3.11% this year. As you can see below, its swap yield steadily rises past 3.5% by 2029. As time progresses and it continues to migrate into higher swap rates, its interest revenue will mechanistically rise. Plus, its swap exposure is lower out the curve, which gives it ample opportunity to steadily leg into swaps at higher yields than its current swap exposure to enhance its overall hedging program.

Separately, noninterest income grew 2% to $625 million led by strength in wealth management. Given elevated market levels, we should see continued strength in results this year while ongoing M&A activity and strong debt issuance should also support capital markets activity. I expect mortgage income to remain relatively muted with the recent tick-up in rates dampening the spring selling season and continuing to offset the strength in other fees.

Looking at credit quality, net charge-offs were 54bps, up 2bps from last year as previously troubled loans were charged off. Nonperforming loans (“NPLs”) are 71bps of the portfolio, and this is down 17bps from last year as it works through old loans and sees limited new delinquencies. During the quarter, it released $39 million of reserves, but it still has $1.65 billion set aside, providing a solid 240% coverage of NPLs. I am comfortable with levels above 200%, and RF meets this threshold.

The company has seen some pressure build among lower income households, though the larger tax refund season has helped to insulate credit quality in the near-term. While there is clearly improving sentiment around the oil situation in the Middle East, if prices remain higher than markets currently are pricing, that inflation shock would likely more than offset the tax refund windfall.

Aside from this, private credit exposure has been a key area of concern. Regions has $13 billion of exposure to nondepository financial institutions (NDFI). It is important to note that not all of this is risky; some are loans to insurance companies for example. Others, like equipment financing and subscription lines, are long-standing bank products with strong track records. Now, only 70% of its loans are investment grade, but even if one assumed a 50% default rate and a 50% loss given default on below investment grade loans, RF’s exposure would be $960 million. This is less than six months of earnings. As such, I do not see this risk creating undue stress at the bank.

Regions ended the quarter with a solid headline tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio of 10.7%. However, if one factors in unrealized losses, its capital ratio would be 9.4%. While RF has time to include this in its calculation, this metric is relevant to the true economic capital position of the bank. I believe RF needs 9.5-10% adjusted capital over the long-term to effectively manage the business, and this is why it has kept headline capital above its targeted operating range. As bonds in its securities portfolio continue to mature, this unrealized loss should decline, bringing capital into a good place this year. Given its solid headline capital ratio and the gradual rolldown of its securities portfolio loss, I view its capital returns sustainable and shares offer a 3.8% dividend yield.

Alongside results, management largely reaffirmed guidance, expecting NIM to exit the year in the low 3.70%’s. Given fixed rate maturities and my expectation for no more than 1 Fed rate cut, I view this NIM guidance as consistent with my own expectation. Additionally, I view low-single-digit loan and deposit growth as reasonable targets given recent performance and size of its pipeline, though I see limited upside opportunity beyond that.

Overall, Regions delivered a solid quarter. While balance sheet growth is muted and likely to remain so, its balance sheet composition is steadily improving thanks to maturities, enhancing the efficiency of its capital, which should support earnings growth. I do expect charge-offs to remain near the high-end of guidance as I am more cautious on the trajectory of inflation. As such, I see RF earning about $2.60 this year. Capital remains a bit below where I like to see on an adjusted basis, so I struggle to see shares moving past 11x (I view 9-12x as the general valuation range for regional banks).

With shares within a couple percent of fair value, I continue to see RF as a “hold.” There is no rush to sell even after such a run, but I do not see enough upside to merit buying. I prefer better capitalized peers like Key (KEY) for greater upside potential.

data.title: Energous: Finally In A Stronger Financial Position And Making Progress With Customers (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It has been nearly three years since I last looked at Energous (WATT). I had covered it for years before that, but the story was fairly similar throughout. Hype (such as rumors of an Apple partnership) far exceeded actual results, as Energous continually reported limited revenue (under $1 million per year from 2018 to 2024) and very heavy cash burn. This resulted in frequent dilution, and Energous ended up doing two reverse splits (a 1-for-20 and a 1-for-30).

However, CEO/CFO Mallorie Burak (who joined Energous in January 2024) appears to have made excellent progress in improving Energous's situation. Energous reported $5.6 million in 2025 revenues (including $3.0 million in Q4 2025) and actually has meaningful product sales now. Operating costs have been significantly reduced and Energous's cash burn appears to be down to around $6 million per year.

Energous's also increased (from equity offerings) its cash position to $39.4 million as of March 23, 2026. This could increase to around $69 million if Energous completes its ATM equity offering program. In either case, Energous now has a long runway.

I am modeling Energous's 2026 revenue at $12 million, although there is a lot of potential variability there. If it can achieve that and make further customer progress, I'd estimate its value at $25 per share (around current levels). I also view Energous's decision to restart quarterly earnings calls as a positive.

Customer Progress

I had noted several years ago that Energous had made progress with proof-of-concept installations and partnerships, but needed to convert those customers to final production.

Energous has since made significant strides, with over 1,500 completed installations at a Fortune 10 customer to help its grocery inventory management and supply chain visibility. This is eventually expected to result in 4,700 installations.

Energous also mentioned that it has over 14 completed installations at a second Fortune 10 customer (e-commerce and reverse logistics) and is separately making progress with proof-of-concept initiatives in a number of different sectors (such as foodservice, retail and manufacturing). It noted that several of those programs are expected to result in near-term rollouts to multiple locations.

2025 Financial Results

Energous reported $5.6 million in revenues for 2025, generating $2 million in gross profit. Energous also reported $12 million in operating expenses for 2025. This is reduced to $10.6 million after excluding items such as severance expense, depreciation and amortization, stock-based compensation and expenses from an abandoned financing transaction.

Energous reported $3.0 million in Q4 2025 revenues, generating $1.1 million in gross profit (approximately 38% gross margins). Meanwhile its adjusted operating expenses were approximately $2.5 million.

These are by far the best results in Energous's history. It reported $2.5 million in revenue in 2015, but then reported under $1.5 million per year in revenue during 2016 and 2017 and under $1 million per year in revenue from 2018 to 2024 (including $0.8 million in 2024).

Energous's operating costs have also been considerably reduced as its adjusted operating expenses were over $30 million back in 2018.

Cash Position And Share Count

Energous has been building its cash position via its ATM equity offering program. It had $64.6 million in remaining availability under that program at the end of 2025 and reported receiving net proceeds of around $31.9 million from equity sales under that program in 2026 (up to March 23). Thus it probably has around $31 million in remaining availability after factoring in commissions and expenses (to get to the gross proceeds number).

Finishing up that ATM program would give Energous around $69 million in proforma cash, up from $39.4 million as of March 23, 2026.

Energous reported having 5.5 million outstanding shares as of March 23, 2026, up from 2.2 million at the end of 2025. This increase reflects the ATM equity sales. If it continues with the ATM equity offering program, it will probably end up with around 7 million to 7.5 million outstanding shares once the availability is used up.

Cash Burn Rate

Energous has spent relatively minimal amounts on purchases of property and equipment recently. Thus its cash burn rate (at Q4 2025 revenue and expense levels) may be approximately $1.5 million per quarter, excluding changes in other working capital items.

Energous appears to have ended up with around $2.9 million in cash burn from operations in Q1 2026 (up to March 23). Some of this was likely due to the $1.1 million in 2025 bonus expense that was planned to be paid out in Q1 2026.

At recent cash burn rates, Energous would have around six years of cash on hand (based on its March 23, 2026 cash position) to around 10 to 11 years of cash on hand (if it completes its ATM equity offering program).

Increased revenue would reduce its cash burn rate, with breakeven cash flow expected at around $30 million per year in revenues. This assumes a high-30s gross margin rate and modest increase in operating expenses with the higher revenues.

Notes On Valuation

I am going to assume that Energous can generate $12 million in 2026 revenues and ends up with $65 million in cash at the end of 2026 after completing its ATM equity offering program.

This revenue level would be 113% above its 2025 revenues although also similar to its annualized Q4 2025 revenues. At this stage, Energous's quarter-to-quarter revenues may be quite variable, due to it being relatively small and currently mainly dependent on one customer for revenues. Energous mentioned that 85% of its 2025 revenues came from one customer, although this percentage should decrease as it advances projects with various other customers. I wouldn't be surprised if its Q1 2026 revenues were lower than its Q4 2025 revenues, although I am assuming that its 2026 revenues average out to $3 million per quarter.

I had mentioned a while back that I believed a 10x revenue multiple could be appropriate for Energous. At $12 million in revenue plus $65 million in cash, that would make Energous worth approximately $185 million, or $25 per share assuming that Energous ends up with around 7.3 million shares.

Conclusion

Energous is in significantly better shape now after years of significant cash burn and dilution. The recent equity sales should give it enough cash on hand to operate into the next decade, especially now that it has reduced operating costs and is finally generating some revenue.

Energous still needs significant further revenue growth (probably to around $30 million per year) to reach breakeven cash flow though. I am modeling its 2026 revenues at $12 million, which would be a more than 100% improvement compared to 2025, although similar to Q4 2025's run rate revenues.

If it can do that, I estimate its value at $25 per share. This also assumes that it can continue making progress with customers (especially outside its main customer).

data.title: Zillow Home Value Index: 'Real' Home Values Continue To Fall
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Zillow, the real estate listing and brokerage website, provides a wealth of publicly available real estate data. Among these, the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) offers a seasonally adjusted measure of home values and market trends. Its "flagship" ZHVI is based on data for "all homes, middle tier, smoothed and seasonally adjusted cut," aiming to represent the typical home value rather than the median. Introduced in 2006, the ZHVI includes various segmentations by geography and housing type. Here, we focus on the flagship ZHVI, which reflects typical home values.

Home values continued to trend higher in March, according to the Zillow Home Value Index. However, after adjusting for inflation, real home values continued to drop, remaining at their lowest level in almost five years. Last month's ZHVI came in at $366,019, up 0.13% from the previous month and up 0.44% from one year ago. After adjusting for inflation, the real figures are -0.15% month-over-month and -2.57% year-over-year.

Zillow Home Value Index Methodology

Zillow’s ZHVI is measured using monthly changes in its “Zestimates,” its proprietary home valuation model. The index is meant to be timely, comprehensive, and transparent, published three weeks after the close of a given month. Many other home value indexes are published with a significant time lag, like the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index and the FHFA House Price Index.

ZHVI is calculated with a few assumptions:

  • Monthly changes are calculated with a weighted mean
  • The average Zestimate within a range determines the index level
  • It can be interpreted as the market’s total appreciation for a given geography or cut

In addition, historical appreciation is based on actual historical housing stock versus a fixed basket of homes, and home improvements are considered in appreciation unrelated to market movements.

The first chart below shows the flagship ZHVI series - the “typical home value” since 2000, not adjusted for inflation. The current nominal value is at an all-time high.

Next, we take a look at the same index values but adjust for inflation using the CPI. As you can see, it’s a different picture. According to the ZHVI, today’s home values are below that of the (inflation-adjusted) housing peak in 2006 and are currently 12.81% off their all-time high from July 2022.

Here is an overlay of the nominal and real indexes for a comparative look.

Housing Prices (ZHVI) and Mortgage Rates

The following charts overlay mortgage rates with both nominal and real ZHVI, highlighting their relationship. Typically, rising interest rates reduce buyer affordability, decreasing demand for homes. This can lead to downward pressure on home prices. With that said, today’s housing market is following a different path because we’re seeing elevated mortgage rates in conjunction with elevated home prices.

Zillow Home Value Index vs. S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index

Zillow claims that the ZHVI is “the most accurate and timely measure of residential real estate prices in the United States" and that its index is more representative of housing stock than the S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index home price index. Zillow claims this is due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction.

Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

© Zillow.com

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are “substantially different from non-distressed sales” and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents “two very different market segments”.

Eric Stubbs, Ph.D. at RBC WM believes foreclosure sales is a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do in fact affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Margin Machine: Why Applied Materials Is Outgrowing Its Peers
data.description:
Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT) has returned a stellar ~185% in the past year, beating the semiconductor industry (SMH), which returned ~135% in the same period. The stock isn't cheap in absolute and relative terms—forward P/E of ~32x against ~23x for the sector. But the stock offers a compelling play to ride the structural tailwinds of AI-driven wafer fab equipment demand as its FY26E revenue is set up to accelerate to a growth rate of 11% Y/Y from FY25's 4.4% Y/Y growth rate.

Financial Summary

Revenue compounded at a 5.3% CAGR from FY2021 to FY2025 due to steady market share gains and equipment intensity expansion. Semi equipment underwent a cyclical digestion from FY2023 to FY2024, reflecting downstream headwinds faced during 2022. However, growth at AMAT was unfazed.

In Q1 2026 (ended January 2026), revenue reached $7.01B, which was down modestly by 2% Y/Y but still managed to beat consensus estimates of about $6.87B as industry investments in AI computing accelerated. EPS of $2.54 also beat estimates of $2.19. Gross margin hit 49%, expanding Q/Q, and is a record high for the company. Its DRAM segment revenues also hit an all-time high, fueled by HBM capacity additions. The company guided revenue to $7.65B +/- $500M in Q2 FY2026E, which at the midpoint implies ~9% Y/Y growth. Non-GAAP EPS is guided to reach $2.64 +/- $0.20. The stock rose ~8% on the day of earnings, reflecting optimism on the beat and raise.

Metric FY2021 FY2022 FY2023 FY2024 FY2025
Revenue ($B) 23.1 25.8 26.5 27.2 28.4
Gross Margin 47.3% 46.5% 46.7% 47.5% 48.7%
Operating Margin 29.9% 30.2% 28.9% 28.9% 29.2%
Net Margin 25.5% 25.3% 25.9% 26.4% 24.7%
Diluted EPS ($) 6.40 7.44 8.11 8.61 8.66
FCF ($B) ~4.8 ~4.6 ~7.6 ~7.5 5.70

Source: Company, Himalayas Research estimates.

AMAT boasts a very generous cash flow—OCF/FCF was $7.96B/$5.70B in FY2025. The company has returned $6.53B in capital back to shareholders that year, consisting of $5.14B in buybacks and $1.38B in dividends, or ~2% on current market cap. The company is also bolstered by a robust net cash position of ~$780M, which leaves dry powder for further share buybacks and dividends. AMAT spends 12.6% of its revenue in FY2025 for R&D, or $3.57B, which is not a low ratio but does underpin its commitment to its technological roadmap.

Forward Estimates

The market expects AMAT to grow at a 13.5% revenue CAGR from FY2025 to FY2028E. And as operating leverage increases, EPS is forecast to grow at a 21.7% CAGR from FY2025 to FY2028E. We believe the AI capex supercycle to structurally support AMAT's growing addressable market. During the latest earnings call, CFO Brice Hill described AI infrastructure investment as set to surge at an unprecedented scale. Hyperscalers capex on AI data centers is projected to spend ~$600B in 2026 before expanding to ~$700B in 2027. This directly translates into demand for leading-edge logic and HBM chips, which leads to more AMAT equipment per wafer as it predominately serves in the advanced nodes market.

Metric FY2025A FY2026E FY2027E FY2028E
Revenue ($B) 28.4 31.5 37.6 41.5
Revenue Growth +4.4% +10.9% ~+19.6% ~+10.3%
Diluted EPS ($) 8.66 11.07 14.01 15.62
EPS Growth ~+0.6% ~+27.8% ~+26.6% ~+11.5%

Source: Company, Market estimates, Himalayas Research estimates.

Positive Catalysts

1. Gate-All-Around (GAA) transistor architecture is a once-in-a-decade inflection for equipment intensity. This process is being deployed at the 2nm node and below by foundries like Intel (INTC), Samsung, and TSMC (TSM). This transition introduces more than 2,000 additional process steps compared to FinFET nodes. Each step requires deposition, etch, or inspection equipment and thus drastically expands AMAT's dollar content per wafer start. At the Morgan Stanley Tech conference in March 2026, the management stated that AMAT has been engaged with ~100 fabs in various stages of development globally.

Further, the company unveiled two new deposition systems purposely developed for angstrom-era logic chips, namely the Precision Selective Nitride PECVD and the Trillium ALD. The two tools address the most integration challenges in 2nm GAA manufacturing, which are selective deposition and atomic-level film control. The cadence of product launch from AMAT supports their ability to tackle markets that have limited direct competition, allowing the company to stay ahead of process inflections and gain equipment market share.

2. AI accelerators rely on high-bandwidth memory (HBM), which is manufactured on advanced DRAM processes that are highly equipment intensive. AMAT's strong DRAM revenue in Q1 FY2026 directly reflects HBM capacity expansions at SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron (MU). In March, CFO Hill signaled "extremely strong" AI chip demand—WFE spending is earmarked to reach $126B in 2026, up significantly from previous years. AMAT is one of the largest WFE suppliers and is disproportionately positioned to capture this market.

3. Durable, long-term business with the Applied Global Services segment. AGS provides installed-base services, spare parts, and software subscriptions to fabs already running AMAT equipment. AGS has expanded into a ~$6B annual run rate with a mid-teens growth rate (~15% Y/Y in Q1 FY2026) and provides a counter-cyclical buffer for the company, as AGS revenue is driven by existing equipment utilization rather than new equipment orders and carries a higher margin.

Key Risks

AMAT has flagged the tightening of U.S. export controls on semiconductor equipment sales to China as a material risk. It was disclosed that ~$710M of expected revenue impact from new export restrictions in FY2026E. On top of that, the company has accrued $252.5M in export compliance matters in Q1 FY2026. China contributed to 40% of revenue at its peak and has now fallen to ~27% today—still significant.

Semiconductor equipment is inherently cyclical. During the last down cycle in 2022-2023, AMAT experienced slower revenue growth in FY2023-FY2024. However, the volatility in revenue/margins during a downturn is meaningfully less than downstream semis.

Despite having a large moat in deposition and implant, competitors span across the entire space and all compete aggressively for process steps in advanced nodes. New entrants from China are gaining share in mature-node markets while remaining far behind in leading-edge technology.

Verdict

Applied Materials is engaged from a structurally advantaged position that benefits from AI-driven semiconductor capital investment, the GAA transistor transition, and HBM memory expansion for AI accelerators.

Against its peer group in semi equipment, AMAT trades cheaply in terms of forward P/E (32x vs. peer average of ~36x) but inline on FCF yield. The compression in forward P/E reflects 26%-27% Y/Y growth rates in EPS during FY2027E and FY2028E. The reason for the increasing operating leverage over peers is that AMAT is more exposed to high-margin nodes for DRAM/HBM and GAA logic, while AGS acts as another high-margin base not readily found at this scale with competitors.

We rate AMAT as a buy, as we believe it can deliver attractive earnings growth through modest revenue growth and increasing margins while not being too expensive in this market.

data.title: Valmont Industries: Management's Plans Trump Short-Term Uncertainty
data.description:
One company that, quite honestly, is doing better than I expected it to right now is Valmont Industries, Inc. (VMI). For those not familiar with the business, it operates as a producer of certain products and a provider of certain services for the infrastructure and agricultural markets. On the infrastructure side, for instance, it offers up structures such as steel, prestressed concrete, and composites dedicated to the utility sector. They also offer up certain structures made of steel, aluminum, wood, and composites for the lighting and transportation markets. They offer up coatings and even provide certain telecommunications equipment. They even cater to the solar industry through their single-axis solar tracker. You also have, on the agricultural side, certain irrigation equipment, not to mention technology solutions. In fact, the company's network has more than 175,000 connected devices that help farmers detect field issues early on while also optimizing water and energy usage. There are other examples, but you get the idea.

In my last article about the company, published in May of 2025, I ended up calling it a ‘hold’ candidate. In retrospect, that was probably a mistake. The fact of the matter is that while the firm looks pretty much fairly valued as it stands right now, it is actually on a very positive track for the next several years. Management has a coherent growth strategy, and achieving that will result in material upside for shareholders. Relative to other similar firms, the stock is also attractively priced right now. In light of all of this, I believe that upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ is the right move.

This is not to say that my opinion on the matter will not change as new data becomes available. It certainly could. It just so happens that, before the market opens on April 21st, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue and profits will improve. I still remain cautious when it comes to the end markets that the company caters to. But on the whole, I think that some optimism is appropriate.

Taking A Fresh Look At Valmont Industries

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the company for the last three fiscal years. Revenue has, quite honestly, been rather lumpy. In fact, in 2025, sales increased only slightly from $4.08 billion to $4.10 billion. We did see a slight improvement in net profits from $348.3 million to $350.3 million. Cash flows, however, were mixed. Operating cash flow declined from $572.7 million to $456.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, on the other hand, we would get a rise from $444.4 million to $538.9 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA ended up pulling back slightly from $647.9 million to $638.8 million.

The best way to understand what is going on here is to look at both of its operating segments separately. The driver of growth from 2024 to 2025 was none other than its Infrastructure segment, which reported a 3% increase in revenue from $3.01 billion to $3.10 billion. Although the company saw weakness in certain categories such as lighting and transportation as well as solar, utility, and telecommunications, demand pushed sales up. Interestingly, North America was an area of strength for the business. International markets, on the other hand, worsened. The utility side of things was particularly robust, with sales jumping 10.4% year over year because of higher volumes and higher prices that the company was able to charge customers. Continued investments in things like data centers and grid capacity have been responsible for investments in electrical energy generation and distribution. This makes perfect sense if you ask me. Even more impressive ended up being the telecommunications side of the business, with revenue skyrocketing 25.2%, with the North American market benefiting from increased investments from carriers and the company snatching up market share through its quick-turn order strategy.

Even though revenue increased, profitability took a hit, with operating income falling from $497.1 million to $430.2 million. The good news is that this was primarily the result of management making the tough decision to incur an impairment charge of $89.4 million associated with its solar and access systems reporting units, as well as certain realignment charges amounting to $7.6 million. These are one-time events and non-cash in nature.

More difficult ended up being financial performance when it came to the Agriculture segment. Driven by an 11.3% plunge in North America, overall revenue for this unit dropped 5.8% from $1.08 billion to $1.02 billion. Management mentioned here that lower irrigation equipment sales reflected continued softness in the agricultural market. It didn't help that lower grain prices, trade policies, and the timing of government funding all negatively impacted its end customers. Some of this was also weather-induced. In 2024, there was an increase in the replacement of irrigation equipment because of severe weather events. That did not recur in 2025.

In the chart above, you can see historical data for the agricultural industry in the US. Total net farm income grew from $127.5 billion to $154.5 billion. This might look great at face value. But the big driver here was a surge in government funding from $10.1 billion to $30.5 billion. Actual crop cash receipts contracted from $244.9 billion to $238.1 billion. That marks the third consecutive year of weakness when it comes to crop receipts. And that is where the company is particularly impacted. Although for 2026, we are expected to see a slight improvement in cash receipts from $238.1 billion to $240.8 billion. Net farm income should fall from $154.5 billion to $153.4 billion. This is in spite of the fact that we should see a further rise of 45.2% when it comes to federal government direct farm program payments.

As revenue for the Agriculture segment dropped, operating profits plunged 33.4%, declining from $138.3 million to $92.1 million. Obviously, lower revenue negatively impacted the company. But in such an asset-intensive space with low margins, it's not unthinkable that the company would experience a contraction in its margins because it has a large amount of fixed assets that revenue must be spread between. Selling, general, and administrative costs also rose because a rise in expected credit losses in Brazil more than offset lower compensation and incentive costs.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management expects revenue to come in at between $4.2 billion and $4.4 billion. Unfortunately, the weakness in the agricultural market should worsen, leading revenue to fall to between $950 million and $1 billion for 2026. Infrastructure revenue, meanwhile, should grow to between $3.25 billion and $3.4 billion. On the whole, however, management expects earnings per share to jump from $20.50 to $23.50. At the midpoint, that would mean profits of $438.6 million. If we scale up other profitability metrics similarly, that would mean adjusted operating cash flow of $674.7 million and EBITDA of about $799.8 million.

Long term, management is optimistic about the company and its prospects. Even though there will be certain challenges in the near term, the firm is targeting continued expansion over the next few years. Between 2028 and 2029, for instance, the business expects revenue to grow by between $500 million and $700 million. In the chart above, you can see what net profits, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA would be for the company at the low end, the midpoint, and the high end if management achieves these targets. This is based on management forecasting earnings per share of between $25 and $30 once these revenue targets are captured.

In the chart above, you can see what the annualized upside for the company would be if management can achieve that in the next three years. That would correspond with 2028. And in the chart below, you can see what the annualized upside would be if management takes four years to get to that goal. While there are scenarios where we would be looking at market returns or even below-market returns, most of these are scenarios where the company slightly exceeds or even comfortably beats the market, which can be expected to appreciate by between 10% and 11% per annum on average.

Relative to other similar companies, the stock is actually decently priced. In the chart below, you can see how the business compares to five other similar enterprises. Although on an absolute basis it is priced in the fair value category, it is cheaper than three of the five firms that I compared it to on a price-to-earnings basis. It's also cheaper than four of the five companies when it comes to both the price-to-operating cash flow approach and the EV-to-EBITDA approach.

Of course, my opinion could change as data justifies it. But the good news is that, at least in the near term, analysts expect further expansion from the company. They believe that the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year will see revenue come in at $995.9 million. That would be up from the $969.3 million the business reported a year earlier. Earnings per share should also grow from $4.32 to $4.67. At the midpoint, this would mean net profits expanding from $87.3 million to $93.1 million. The table below shows certain cash flow metrics for the first quarter of 2025. In all likelihood, seeing revenue and profitability expand would mean experiencing a rise in most or all of these as well.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I would say that my previous stance on Valmont Industries was incorrect. The company is actually doing quite well considering how difficult the agricultural industry is. The stock is not exactly cheap right now. But if management can achieve the upside in terms of revenue and profitability it is forecasting, I would argue that shares justify enough upside to warrant a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: Sandisk: A Supercycle Is Still A Cycle
data.description:
From DRAM To NAND Flash Memory

After updating my outlook on Micron (MU) and the DRAM market in February 2026, I now turn my attention to Sandisk Corporation (SNDK), a pure-play developer and manufacturer of NAND flash memory. As indicated in the table below, DRAM represented 79% of Micron's total revenues in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, with NAND accounting for the remaining 21%. This is the key difference between Micron and Sandisk, as the latter is entirely focused on the NAND flash memory market.

The DRAM market is well-known for its deeply cyclical behavior and abrupt market turns. At the end of the day, DRAM is a commodity that goes from periods of oversupply to periods of shortages, as is the case today. There is no question that the DRAM market is in shortage today, but the real question is how long will it remain so?

So what is the difference between DRAM and NAND memory? To put it simply, NAND memory is used to store data, while DRAM becomes useful when retrieving that stored data. DRAM is a volatile form of memory, meaning that it loses its data when power is turned off, while it is the opposite for NAND. This is precisely why NAND memory is used in applications like solid-state drives, smartphones, USB storage devices, and SD cards, among many others.

In terms of market size, the NAND memory market is much smaller when compared to DRAM, even though they are both growing at a rapid pace. More precisely, the size of the global NAND memory market is expected to reach $58.7 billion in 2026, compared to an estimated $124.4 billion for the DRAM market. Given the significant surge in demand associated with the ongoing AI infrastructure boom, both markets are in a state of severe shortage, as reflected by surging spot prices across the board. Memory is now a critical bottleneck in the global AI data center supply chain, which allows both DRAM and NAND manufacturers to report all-time-high margins and profits.

In a sense, the global NAND and DRAM markets are highly similar in terms of global market share, with Samsung and SK Hynix dominating both industries. It is only when looking at the smaller players that the market structure starts to change. More specifically, the two largest manufacturers represented 49% and 68% of the global NAND and DRAM markets as of Q4-25, respectively. Despite its exclusive focus on the NAND market, Sandisk held a market share of only ~13% as of the fourth quarter of 2025, with its key joint-venture partner Kioxia slightly ahead at ~15%. Another point that I would like to highlight is the fact that Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron all have vertically integrated supply chains with in-house fabs.

While Sandisk's partnership with Kioxia helps in lowering capital intensity, this strategy also carries drawbacks, especially in times of shortages like we are experiencing today. It is difficult to understate how critically important the partnership with Kioxia is for Sandisk. This collaboration takes place within an entity called Flash Ventures, owned at 49.9% by Sandisk. Key terms of the joint-venture agreement come to expiry as early as December 2029. As per Sandisk's 2025 10-K filing:

Kioxia owns the facilities and provides wafer manufacturing services to Flash Ventures at cost using manufacturing equipment owned or leased by Flash Ventures and process technologies co-owned or contributed by us and Kioxia. Flash Ventures accounts for approximately 80% of the total manufacturing capacity in the facilities owned by Kioxia.

In short, the NAND flash memory market is much smaller in size and also a lot more fragmented when compared to the DRAM market. Despite its pure-play exposure, Sandisk is the second smallest player in the industry, only ahead of Yangtze Memory Technologies, China's largest NAND manufacturer. Given its aggressive expansion plan, I would also expect YMTC to surpass Sandisk in terms of size over the next few quarters. As reported by Reuters on April 14th, the Chinese manufacturer is in the process of building three additional facilities, with each having a production capacity of 100,000 wafers per month. To put this number of 300,000 additional wafers per month into perspective, YMTC is currently operating two manufacturing facilities, which have a combined capacity of 200,000 wafers per month. With this 150% increase in production capacity, YMTC could leap ahead of Sandisk, Micron, and Kioxia once all the new facilities come online. The first factory is expected to start production later this year, reaching a capacity of 50,000 wafers per month in 2027. Although it is still a relatively small amount, it shows that Chinese manufacturers are serious about gaining market share, and that is true for both NAND and DRAM.

The S&P 500 Superstar

Over the last few weeks, I have spent quite a bit of time researching beaten-down names like Novo Nordisk (NVO), Copart (CPRT), ServiceNow(NOW), and Salesforce (CRM), among others. By looking into Sandisk today, I am doing the exact opposite, as the stock has been the S&P 500's best performer in both 2025 and 2026 year-to-date. In 2025 alone, Sandisk surged by an impressive 559%, on top of adding another 285% so far in 2026. When it started to trade as an independent entity back in February 2025, Sandisk was initially trading around $35.00 per share. Using today's share price of around $920.00, Sandisk has now delivered total returns in excess of 2,500% over a period of just 14 months or so. This is truly impressive, indeed.

As a reminder, Western Digital (WDC) announced its intention to separate into two publicly listed entities back in October 2023. This strategic decision came after the activist firm Elliott Management disclosed a stake in Western Digital in November 2022 and later put pressure on WDC's board of directors to explore such a separation. If we go further back in time, we also find that Western Digital completed the acquisition of Sandisk in May 2016, a deal worth ~$15.8 billion at closing. The timing of the transaction came just before the significant upswing in NAND and DRAM demand starting in 2017 and peaking in the following year, in 2018. To provide additional context, Sandisk is currently trading at a $135.6 billion market capitalization, which is ~8.6x higher than what Western Digital paid a decade ago.

Following the completion of Sandisk's spin-off in February 2025, Western Digital retained a 19.9% stake in the company. In early June 2025, WDC disposed of another large block of shares, representing 14.6% of SNDK's outstanding shares. As of early January 2026, Western Digital retained 7.5 million shares, or the equivalent of a 5.1% stake. More recently, in February 2026, Western Digital announced another disposition of 5.8 million shares at a public offering price of $545.00 per share. As of now, WDC still holds approximately 1.7 million Sandisk shares with the intention of completing the divestiture in subsequent transactions.

At the end of the day, how much value was truly created by Elliott's involvement for WDC's shareholders? Some could argue that no value was lost whatsoever if investors in Western Digital simply kept their shares of the spin-off, but how many really did so? Without questioning the initial rationale behind the separation, the 19.9% stake Western Digital retained following the spin-off would still be worth ~$27.0 billion as of now, or roughly 22% of WDC's current market capitalization of ~$122.6 billion. In terms of raw market capitalization, that is to say that the spin-off has now become larger than the parent.

I would like to conclude this section by saying that all the beaten-down stocks mentioned previously were all market darlings at some point. For example, Novo Nordisk (NVO) went from a top performer in 2023 and 2024 to being hated as of right now, with a drawdown from peak that has now reached ~75%. From market darling to the garbage bin in roughly two years. In previous articles, I have told a similar story about many other companies, with Trade Desk (TTD) and Brown-Forman (BF.B) being great examples. Yes indeed, they all operate in completely different industries with unique economics, but the core underlying pattern is the same, which is the cyclical nature of everything in financial markets. As Edward Dewey wrote in his book 'Cycles: The Science Of Prediction':

Anyone who starts out in business without knowing what time it is on the economic clock will obviously owe much to luck if he succeeds. And many men who have engaged successfully in business for a long time owe much of their sheer good fortune in having started out when the economic sun was rising.

Compared to all of those former darlings, Sandisk operates in an industry notorious for its deeply cyclical nature and abrupt market turns. There is no question that the NAND flash memory market is currently in a state of severe shortage, given the surging demand associated with the ongoing and historic AI infrastructure boom. How long is it going to last? And how long does it have to last to justify Sandisk's current market capitalization of $136.5 billion?

Using the Excel add-on Wisesheets, I have compiled Sandisk's historical financial statements going all the way back to fiscal 2022, when it was still a subsidiary of Western Digital. While the company is currently reaping the benefits associated with the AI supercycle, I think it is also important to keep in mind what the financial picture may look like in the midst of a downturn, like the one experienced over the last few years.
